---
title: "Chapter 29 — 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 28 · 💬 Natural Language Processing](ch28.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [30 · 📚 Large Language Models →](ch30.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Speech and audio processing is the part of AI that turns sound — pressure waves in the air — into something a machine can recognize, label, or generate. It spans recognizing spoken words, synthesizing natural voices, telling speakers apart, and finding structure in music and environmental sound. It sits at the intersection of digital signal processing and deep learning, and it underpins voice assistants, captioning, dictation, audiobooks, and music apps.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Language, Vision & Modalities · used for speech recognition, voice synthesis, speaker and music analysis · the one key idea: sound is a one-dimensional sequence, and almost everything starts by turning it into a time–frequency image.
:::

## 29.1 — Sound as a signal and a sequence

Start with the physical picture. A sound is a vibration that compresses and rarefies the air; a microphone measures that pressure over time and a converter samples it into numbers. The result is a **waveform**: a long list of amplitude values, one per sample. To capture speech faithfully you typically sample at 16,000 times per second (16 kHz); music is usually 44.1 kHz. The **sampling rate** matters because of the **Nyquist limit** — you can only represent frequencies up to half the sampling rate, so 16 kHz captures everything up to 8 kHz, which covers the bulk of speech intelligibility. Sample above the Nyquist rate and high frequencies *fold back* as false low ones — an artifact called **aliasing**, which is why a recorder filters out frequencies above the limit before sampling.

The catch is that the raw waveform is enormous and awkward. One second of 16 kHz audio is 16,000 numbers, and a single spoken word smears across thousands of them. The information humans actually hear — pitch, timbre, which vowel is being spoken — lives not in any single sample but in the *patterns of oscillation*, that is, in the **frequencies** present and how they change over time.

This is why audio is treated as a **sequence**. Like text or video, it is ordered in time, has long-range structure, and is best modeled by architectures built for sequences (recurrent networks, and now transformers). But unlike text, the natural unit is not a discrete token — it is a continuous signal, so the first job is always to choose a representation.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: almost no modern system feeds raw waveform straight into a classifier. The standard move is to convert sound into a time–frequency picture first, because frequency content is where the meaning lives.
:::

## 29.2 — Audio representations: spectrogram, mel-spectrogram, MFCCs

The bridge from waveform to "frequency content over time" is the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**. The intuition: a long sound changes too much to analyze all at once, so you chop it into short overlapping windows (say 25 ms each, hopping forward 10 ms at a time), and within each window you ask "what frequencies are present?" using the **Fourier transform** — the mathematical operation that decomposes a signal into the sum of pure sine waves that make it up. Each window is short enough that the sound is roughly steady, so the question has a clean answer.

There is a fundamental tension here, the **time–frequency tradeoff**. A long window pins down frequency precisely but blurs *when* things happened; a short window localizes events in time but smears frequency. You cannot have both at once — this is the signal-processing echo of an uncertainty principle, and choosing the window length is choosing where to sit on that tradeoff.

Stack the per-window answers side by side and you get a **spectrogram**: a 2-D image where the horizontal axis is time, the vertical axis is frequency, and brightness is energy at that frequency and moment. A rising whistle becomes a diagonal streak; a vowel becomes horizontal bands (its **formants**, the resonant frequencies of the vocal tract that distinguish one vowel from another).

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Waveform] -->|frame into<br/>25ms windows| B[Windowed frames]
  B -->|FFT per frame| C[Spectrogram<br/>linear freq]
  C -->|mel filterbank| D[Mel-spectrogram]
  D -->|log + DCT| E[MFCCs]
```

Two refinements make the spectrogram match human hearing. First, our ears resolve low frequencies finely and high frequencies coarsely; the **mel scale** warps the frequency axis to mimic this, and applying a bank of overlapping triangular filters produces a **mel-spectrogram** — far fewer rows, perceptually weighted. Second, taking the logarithm of the energies (we hear loudness logarithmically) and then a **discrete cosine transform (DCT)** decorrelates the result into a compact set of coefficients called **MFCCs** (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients), usually 13 per frame.

The lineage is one of increasing compression and increasing "match to perception":

| Representation | Size per frame | What it keeps | Typical use |
|----------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Waveform | raw samples | everything | neural vocoders, end-to-end models |
| Spectrogram (STFT) | ~hundreds | full frequency detail | source separation, analysis |
| Mel-spectrogram | 40–128 | perceptual frequency bands | modern deep ASR / TTS input |
| MFCCs | ~13 | compact decorrelated envelope | classical ASR, lightweight tasks |

Here is the mechanism, from scratch, for a magnitude spectrogram — the core of all of the above:

```python
import numpy as np

def spectrogram(x, win=400, hop=160):
    # win=25ms, hop=10ms at 16kHz
    window = np.hanning(win)            # taper to reduce edge artifacts
    frames = []
    for start in range(0, len(x) - win, hop):
        seg = x[start:start+win] * window
        spec = np.fft.rfft(seg)        # real FFT -> complex spectrum
        frames.append(np.abs(spec))    # magnitude = energy per frequency
    return np.array(frames).T          # shape: [freq_bins, time_frames]

# self-check: a 1kHz tone should peak in the right frequency bin
sr = 16000
t = np.arange(sr) / sr
tone = np.sin(2 * np.pi * 1000 * t)
S = spectrogram(tone)
peak_bin = S.mean(axis=1).argmax()
peak_hz = peak_bin * sr / 400
assert abs(peak_hz - 1000) < 60, peak_hz
print("peak at", round(peak_hz), "Hz")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
MFCCs were dominant in the HMM era and are still great when compute is tight, but the log-mel-spectrogram threw away less and won. Deep models prefer mel-spectrograms (or even raw waveform) because the DCT step in MFCCs discards correlations a neural network would rather exploit itself.
:::

## 29.3 — Automatic Speech Recognition: from pipelines to end-to-end

**Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** maps audio to text. The hard part is that the alignment is unknown: you do not know which slice of audio produced which letter, the same word stretches or compresses depending on speaking rate, and acoustics are ambiguous ("recognize speech" vs. "wreck a nice beach").

The **classical pipeline** decomposed the problem into pieces that each modeled one source of knowledge:

- an **acoustic model** (historically a Gaussian-mixture **HMM**, a Hidden Markov Model that treats speech as passing through hidden phonetic states over time) scoring how well audio frames match phonemes,
- a **pronunciation lexicon** mapping words to phoneme sequences,
- a **language model** scoring how plausible a word sequence is as text.

A decoder searched for the word sequence maximizing $P(\text{words}\mid\text{audio}) \propto P(\text{audio}\mid\text{words})\,P(\text{words})$ — acoustic likelihood times language-model prior, straight from Bayes' rule. This worked but was brittle: every component was trained separately and the lexicon had to be hand-built.

**End-to-end ASR** collapses the whole stack into one neural network trained directly on (audio, text) pairs. Three flavors dominate:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[Audio frames] --> B[Encoder<br/>conv + transformer]
  B --> C{Decoding head}
  C -->|CTC| D[Frame labels<br/>collapse blanks]
  C -->|Attention seq2seq| E[Decode text<br/>token by token]
  C -->|Transducer RNN-T| F[Streaming<br/>align + emit]
```

The alignment problem is solved most elegantly by **CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification)**, covered in depth in the sequence-models chapter. The idea: let the network emit a label (or a special **blank**) at every frame, then define the probability of a transcript as the sum over all frame-level alignments that collapse to it (merge repeats, drop blanks). Training maximizes that summed probability, so you never need a per-frame label.

The other two heads trade off differently. An **attention sequence-to-sequence** decoder generates text one token at a time, attending freely over the whole encoded utterance — accurate, but it likes to see the entire input before committing, which hurts live use. A **transducer (RNN-T)** keeps a separate predictor over the text emitted so far and combines it with the audio encoder, emitting symbols incrementally; this makes it the natural fit for streaming, as §29.7 revisits.

**Whisper-style models** are the current mainstream: a transformer encoder reads a log-mel-spectrogram, a transformer decoder generates text, and the whole thing is trained on enormous, weakly-labeled multilingual audio scraped from the web. The payoff of that scale is robustness — it handles accents, background noise, and many languages without per-domain tuning, and it can transcribe and translate in one model.

Quality is reported as **Word Error Rate (WER)**: the number of word substitutions, insertions, and deletions needed to turn the model's transcript into the reference, divided by the number of reference words. Lower is better; $0$ is a perfect transcript, and a WER near or above $1$ means the output is essentially useless.

::: {.callout-tip}
A separate language model still helps even end-to-end systems. **Shallow fusion** — adding a language model's log-probability to the acoustic score during beam search — cheaply injects fluency and domain vocabulary the audio model never saw.
:::

## 29.4 — Text-to-Speech: from concatenation to neural vocoders

**Text-to-Speech (TTS)** runs ASR backwards: text in, natural-sounding audio out. The challenge is that text is wildly underspecified — it says *what* to say but not the pitch, rhythm, emphasis, or emotion (**prosody**) that make speech sound human rather than robotic. The same sentence can be a calm statement or an incredulous question, and the letters alone do not say which.

The field evolved through three eras:

- **Concatenative** TTS recorded a voice actor for hours, sliced the audio into tiny units (phonemes, **diphones** — transitions from one phoneme into the next), and at synthesis time stitched the best-matching units together. It could sound remarkably natural in-distribution but was inflexible — you could not change the voice or emotion without re-recording — and the joins between units often produced audible glitches.
- **Parametric** (statistical) TTS instead predicted acoustic features (a sequence of mel-spectrogram-like vectors) from text with a statistical model, then ran a **vocoder** to convert those features back to a waveform. It was flexible and small but sounded muffled and buzzy because the vocoders were crude.
- **Neural** TTS combined the two ideas with deep networks and won decisively.

Modern neural TTS is a two-stage pipeline. First an **acoustic model** like **Tacotron** (an attention-based sequence-to-sequence network) maps a character or phoneme sequence to a mel-spectrogram, learning prosody implicitly from data. Then a **neural vocoder** turns that mel-spectrogram into a waveform.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  T[Text /<br/>phonemes] --> AM[Acoustic model<br/>e.g. Tacotron] --> M[Mel-spectrogram] --> V[Neural vocoder<br/>e.g. WaveNet] --> W[Waveform]
```

The vocoder is where the magic happens. **WaveNet** was the breakthrough: an **autoregressive** model that predicts each audio sample one at a time, conditioned on all previous samples, using stacked **dilated causal convolutions** — convolutions that skip over inputs with growing gaps so a modest stack of layers can see thousands of samples back in time while only looking at the past. The output quality was unprecedented, but generating 16,000+ samples per second one-by-one is painfully slow. Later vocoders (parallel and GAN-based) kept the quality while generating in parallel, making real-time synthesis practical.

How do you score a synthetic voice when there is no single right answer? The standard yardstick is the **Mean Opinion Score (MOS)**: human listeners rate samples on a 1-to-5 naturalness scale and you average the ratings. It is subjective and expensive but remains the gold standard, because no automatic metric fully captures whether speech sounds human.

::: {.callout-warning}
Naturalness is not the only axis. A vocoder can sound gorgeous yet hallucinate prosody, mispronounce names, or run too slowly for a live assistant. TTS systems are judged on a triangle of quality, controllability, and latency, and you rarely get all three for free.
:::

## 29.5 — Speaker identification and diarization

So far the question was *what* was said. A different family asks *who* said it. The key tool is the **speaker embedding**: a neural network (trained on many speakers) maps an utterance to a fixed-length vector — a "voiceprint" — such that two recordings of the same person land close together and different people land far apart, regardless of the words spoken. The training trick is **metric learning**: the network is rewarded for pulling same-speaker pairs together and pushing different-speaker pairs apart, so the geometry of the embedding space *becomes* speaker identity.

With embeddings, several tasks fall out of simple geometry:

- **Speaker verification** (1:1): is this the claimed person? Compute the cosine similarity between a new embedding and the enrolled one; accept if above a threshold. This is how "Hey, it's me" voice unlock works.
- **Speaker identification** (1:N): which of N known people is this? Pick the nearest enrolled embedding.
- **Speaker diarization**: in a multi-person recording with no enrollment, answer "who spoke when?" — segment the audio, embed each segment, and **cluster** the embeddings so each cluster is one speaker.

<svg viewBox="0 0 300 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <circle cx="70" cy="50" r="6" fill="#4f8cff"/><circle cx="85" cy="62" r="6" fill="#4f8cff"/><circle cx="62" cy="68" r="6" fill="#4f8cff"/>
  <text x="55" y="95" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Speaker A</text>
  <circle cx="210" cy="60" r="6" fill="#ff6b6b"/><circle cx="225" cy="48" r="6" fill="#ff6b6b"/><circle cx="230" cy="70" r="6" fill="#ff6b6b"/>
  <text x="195" y="100" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Speaker B</text>
  <text x="90" y="20" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">embedding space</text>
</svg>

Diarization is genuinely hard: speakers overlap, the number of speakers is often unknown, and short segments give noisy embeddings. It is the engine behind meeting transcripts that label each line with a name, and it is usually run alongside ASR — one model decides *what*, the other decides *who*, and the outputs are merged.

```python
import numpy as np

def cosine(a, b):
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

# toy: two enrolled voiceprints, classify a new utterance
alice = np.array([0.9, 0.1, 0.2])
bob   = np.array([0.1, 0.8, 0.3])
new   = np.array([0.85, 0.15, 0.25])   # sounds like alice
who = "alice" if cosine(new, alice) > cosine(new, bob) else "bob"
assert who == "alice"
print(who, round(cosine(new, alice), 3))
```

::: {.callout-warning}
A speaker embedding is biometric data. Voiceprints can identify a person across recordings, so treat enrollment vectors with the same care as fingerprints — and remember that modern voice cloning can forge a convincing match from seconds of audio, which is why verification alone is weak security.
:::

## 29.6 — Audio classification, music information retrieval, and keyword spotting

Not all audio is speech. A large family of tasks asks for a **label on a sound**: is that a dog bark, a gunshot, a smoke alarm (**audio event detection**)? What genre, key, or tempo is this song (**music information retrieval, MIR**)? The recipe is strikingly uniform — turn the audio into a (mel-)spectrogram and treat it as an image for a convolutional or transformer classifier. A spectrogram really is an image: local patterns (a snare hit, a phoneme onset) are exactly what convolutions detect, which is why vision architectures transfer so well to audio.

MIR has its own characteristic problems: **beat tracking** (finding the pulse), **chord recognition**, **key detection**, **onset detection** (when notes start), and **music tagging** (mood, instrument, genre). Many reduce to finding periodicity or transitions in the spectrogram over time.

A special, deployment-critical case is **wake-word detection** (a.k.a. **keyword spotting**) — the "Hey Siri" / "Alexa" trigger. The constraints flip everything: the model must run continuously on a tiny always-on chip, drain almost no battery, respond in milliseconds, and almost never false-trigger. So instead of a large transcriber, you run a tiny classifier on a short rolling audio window that outputs only "wake word: yes/no." Only after it fires does the heavy cloud ASR wake up. The design tension is the **precision–recall** balance: trigger too eagerly and the device wakes at random (false positives); trigger too cautiously and it ignores the user (false negatives) — and because the model listens all day, even a tiny per-second false-positive rate adds up fast.

| Task | Output | Where it runs | Key constraint |
|------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Wake-word spotting | yes/no for one phrase | on-device, always on | tiny, low-power, low false-alarm |
| Full ASR | open-vocabulary text | cloud or large on-device | accuracy, vocabulary |
| Audio event detection | sound-class label(s) | varies | many classes, noisy |
| Music tagging / MIR | genre, key, tempo, tags | offline / server | musical structure |

::: {.callout-tip}
The "spectrogram-as-image + CNN/transformer" pattern is the workhorse of non-speech audio. If you can frame your audio problem as classifying or detecting patterns in a mel-spectrogram, you can reuse the entire computer-vision toolbox.
:::

## 29.7 — Self-supervised audio representations

Labeled audio is scarce and expensive — transcribing speech by hand is slow, and most of the world's recordings have no transcript at all. **Self-supervised learning** sidesteps this by training on raw, unlabeled audio first, inventing a task the audio can answer about itself, and only fine-tuning on a small labeled set afterward. It is the audio analogue of how large language models pre-train on raw text.

The dominant idea is **masked prediction**, made famous by models like **wav2vec 2.0** and **HuBERT**. The intuition mirrors fill-in-the-blank: hide chunks of the audio and make the model predict what belonged there from the surrounding context. To predict a masked slice well, the network must learn what phonemes and sounds *are* — so it builds rich speech representations without ever seeing a transcript.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Raw waveform] --> B[CNN feature<br/>encoder]
  B --> C[Mask random<br/>spans]
  C --> D[Transformer<br/>context network]
  D --> E[Predict masked<br/>content]
  D -.fine-tune.-> F[ASR / tasks<br/>tiny labeled set]
```

The payoff is data efficiency. A model pre-trained on thousands of hours of unlabeled audio can then reach strong ASR accuracy after fine-tuning on as little as ten minutes of transcribed speech — a regime where training from scratch would fail completely. This is also why low-resource languages, with little transcribed data, benefit enormously: the heavy lifting happens on cheap unlabeled audio. These learned representations now serve as the front end for ASR, speaker, and audio-classification systems alike, increasingly replacing hand-designed MFCC and mel features.

::: {.callout-tip}
When you have a mountain of audio but almost no labels, reach for a pre-trained self-supervised encoder before designing features by hand. Fine-tuning a wav2vec/HuBERT-style backbone usually beats anything you build from MFCCs on the same small labeled set.
:::

## 29.8 — Persistent challenges: noise, accents, and latency

Audio systems that ace a clean benchmark can collapse in the real world, and it is worth knowing the standard failure modes.

**Noise and reverberation.** Background chatter, traffic, music, and room echo all corrupt the signal. The defenses are **data augmentation** (training on audio with synthetic noise, room reverb, and time/frequency masking of the spectrogram so the model learns to be robust), front-end **speech enhancement** or **source separation** (isolating the target voice before recognition — the classic framing is the *cocktail-party problem* of following one voice in a crowded room), and **multi-microphone beamforming** (using an array of mics to spatially focus on the speaker).

**Accents, dialects, and code-switching.** A model trained mostly on one accent underperforms on others, and many real speakers mix languages mid-sentence (**code-switching**). This is partly a data-coverage problem — the cure is broad, diverse training data — and large weakly-supervised models like Whisper improved here mainly by scaling that diversity.

**Latency, especially streaming.** A dictation app can wait for you to finish a sentence, but a live captioner or voice assistant must emit words as you speak. **Streaming** ASR must commit to outputs from partial audio, which is why architectures like the **RNN-Transducer** — designed to align and emit incrementally — are preferred over attention models that like to see the whole utterance before decoding. There is a genuine tradeoff: smaller look-ahead means lower latency but usually lower accuracy.

::: {.callout-warning}
Word Error Rate on a clean read-speech test set is a flattering number. Production audio adds far-field microphones, overlapping speakers, domain-specific jargon, and a latency budget — evaluate on conditions that match deployment, not on the leaderboard set.
:::

## 29.9 — Key takeaways

- Sound is a 1-D time sequence; the first and most important step is almost always converting the **waveform** into a time–frequency representation, mindful of the **Nyquist limit** and the **time–frequency tradeoff**.
- The representation ladder is **spectrogram → mel-spectrogram → MFCCs**, trading detail for perceptual compactness; deep models favor **log-mel-spectrograms** (or raw waveform), classical systems used MFCCs.
- **ASR** moved from separate acoustic + lexicon + language models to **end-to-end** networks (**CTC**, attention seq2seq, transducers); Whisper-style large weakly-supervised transformers are today's robust default, scored by **Word Error Rate**.
- **TTS** evolved concatenative → parametric → **neural**, now a two-stage acoustic model (**Tacotron**) plus **neural vocoder** (**WaveNet** and faster successors); judged on quality, controllability, and latency, with naturalness measured by **MOS**.
- **Speaker embeddings** turn "who spoke" into geometry — enabling verification, identification, and **diarization** by clustering; they are biometric data and forgeable by voice cloning.
- **Self-supervised** models (**wav2vec 2.0**, **HuBERT**) pre-train on unlabeled audio via masked prediction, then fine-tune on tiny labeled sets — the key to low-resource speech.
- Non-speech audio (events, **MIR**, **wake-word spotting**) mostly reduces to **CNN/transformer-on-spectrogram**, with wake-word models squeezed for tiny size and low power.
- The hard, persistent problems are **noise/reverb, accents/code-switching, and streaming latency** — addressed by augmentation, enhancement, diverse data, and incremental architectures.

## 29.10 — See also

- **Chapter 17 — Recurrent & Sequence Models** (CTC, RNN-Transducer, the sequence-modeling backbone of ASR)
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** (the encoder–decoder architecture behind Whisper and Tacotron)
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** (language models for fusion, the text side of ASR/TTS)
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models** (scaling and weak supervision, increasingly merged with speech)
- **Chapter 34 — Multimodal AI** (joint audio-text-vision models and speech-to-speech systems)
- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks** (spectrogram-as-image classification, dilated convolutions in WaveNet)
- **Chapter 09 — Clustering & Unsupervised Learning** (the clustering step inside speaker diarization)
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** (signal framing, windowing, and augmentation)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 28 · 💬 Natural Language Processing](ch28.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [30 · 📚 Large Language Models →](ch30.html)
